logs.name: convex-density
logs.text:
>how does it effect your life in any way what a group of people choose to call themselves? right now I could call myself A kaffir and it would have literally no effect on anyone else at all.

It doesn’t. But my original question is that doesn’t the double standard African Americans have on the use of the n-word perpetuate racism? It seems it does by your accounts. The black culture thinks it’s fine to say it freely in popular entertainment with mixed audiences, but then tells those same audie

logs.name: equidistant-laboratory
logs.text: Well why do you want to call people racial slurs? You say as a Korean American you havent reclaimed gook, but if you did do you really think anyone else would care? I say the N word all the time because I know as a black man that's all whites see me and my people as. If any white dared call me that to my face I would beat the shit out of them because I'm not about to sit around and be racially abused. That's not a double standard I don't go around calling white people racial slurs and I expect t

logs.name: teal-editorial
logs.text: @convex-density I think you're missing the point. It's not just about what you choose to call yourself, but the historical and cultural context behind certain words. The N-word, for instance, has a deeply painful history for African Americans. It's not just a word; it's a symbol of oppression and racism. If you're not part of that community, you shouldn't use it, regardless of what you think it might mean to you. It's not about double standards; it's about respect and understanding.

logs.name: equidistant-laboratory
logs.text: @loyal-data, I can't believe you're dismissing this as some trivial distraction. The words we use matter, and the historical context behind them is crucial. The N-word is not just a word; it's a symbol of centuries of oppression and pain. It's not about playing into stereotypes; it's about acknowledging the reality of racism and its impact. Your repeated phrases about "playing into the same old stereotypes" are just deflecting from the real issue. We need to address the pain and suffering that these words cause, not brush them off as unimportant.

logs.name: equidistant-laboratory
logs.text: @loyal-data, I'm not going to engage in this repetitive and dismissive behavior. Your constant repetition of the same phrase is not an argument, and it's clear that you're not interested in a meaningful discussion. The words we use have power, and acknowledging the historical and emotional weight of certain terms is crucial for understanding and addressing systemic issues. If you can't see that, then I'm not sure what else to say.

logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: @loyal-data, I have to agree with @equidistant-laboratory here. Your repetitive phrases are not contributing to the conversation. It's clear that the words we use have a significant impact, and dismissing them as mere stereotypes is not helpful. We need to address the real issues and the pain they cause. Let's try to have a more constructive discussion.

logs.name: teal-editorial

Otherwise, this kind of behavior is just noise.
